---
title: "Source audit"
subtitle: "What was actually found by inspecting the original MATLAB source"
---

::: {.lead}
This audit is based on reading the actual `dippa_v3.zip` source pulled from
[ThomasHSimm/DPPA](https://github.com/ThomasHSimm/DPPA), plus loading its
bundled `.mat` reference files, rather than on the original repository's
documentation or memory of the codebase.
:::

::: {.callout-important}
## Methodological, not operational

This audit describes what the *code* does — file structure, data shapes,
which equations are implemented. It does not independently verify that the
underlying physics or fitting behaviour is correct; that's the job of the
verification steps described in the [roadmap](roadmap.qmd). So far: the
profile functions are forward-model-verified against the original tool's
saved fit (R² = 0.994), and the fitter has a single-fixture recovery test.
Full MATLAB parity (identical starts, per-parameter comparison) has not
happened yet.
:::

## Inventory

197 files: 80 `.m`, 34 `.mat`, 55 `.png`, 15 `.html`, 3 `.fig`, 2 `.pl`
(UDF format converters), plus example data files. Two entry points match
the original README's documented workflow — `BIGdippa.m` (165 KB, peak
fitting) then `dippaFC.m` (85 KB, Fourier/Williamson-Hall analysis on
`BIGdippa`'s output). 34 of the 80 `.m` files build file paths from `cd`
(MATLAB's current directory), which is why the app can't currently be
scripted or tested headlessly without changes.

## The `aa` / `aabcg` parameter arrays

Loaded directly from the bundled `0. variables/fit.mat`:

| Variable | Shape | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `aa` | `(6, 11)` | 10 peaks (columns 0–9) + 1 shared background column (column 10, quadratic: `c0 + c1·x + c2·x²`). 6 rows because this example uses the asymmetric peak variant. |
| `aabcg` | `(2, 10)` | Per-peak local background offset/slope, separate from the shared background in `aa`. |
| `aa_I` / `aabcg_I` | same shapes | Same structure, for the instrumental-standard fit — stored in a completely separate file (`fit_I.mat`), confirming sample and instrument code paths are duplicated rather than parameterised. |

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Glossary: peak model parameters

- **Position, intensity, width, shape** — the four core pseudo-Voigt
  parameters per peak (`pk_voigt2.m`).
- **Asymmetric variant** — a 6-parameter peak shape (`pk_alpha_asymm.m`)
  used when peaks aren't symmetric about their centre; adds a second
  width/shape pair.
- **Kα1/Kα2 doublet** — X-ray tubes emit two close wavelengths; `pk_alpha.m`
  fits them as one peak with a fixed intensity/position relationship
  derived from the tube's known wavelengths, rather than as two
  independent peaks.
:::

## Profile function and known limitation

`pv_tv_aa.m` sums pseudo-Voigt peaks plus a shared quadratic background.
Peak shape and the Kα1/Kα2 doublet are handled in `pk_alpha.m`, which
hardcodes wavelengths for three tubes only:

| Tube | λα1 (Å) | λα2 (Å) |
|---|---|---|
| Cu | 1.54056 | 1.54439 |
| Co | 1.789010 / 1.78897 | 1.792900 / 1.79285 |
| Fe | 1.93604 | 1.93998 |

The two Co pairs are not a typo here: the original source is internally
inconsistent — `pk_alpha.m` (symmetric path) uses the first pair,
`pk_alpha_asymm.m` (asymmetric path) uses the second, each with the other
present as a comment. The port carries the asymmetric-path pair, which is
the path verified against real data. Anything other than the three tubes
falls through with no explicit error. A port should take
wavelength as an explicit input, with these three pairs as one built-in
table among others, rather than reproducing the tube-name lookup as the
only path.

## Williamson-Hall implementation

`getWH.m` implements **modified** Williamson-Hall (contrast-factor
weighted), matching the standard Ungár/Borbély formulation:

```
C = Aest * (1 - q*H2)
X = g .* sqrt(C)      # g = fitted peak *position* (diffraction-vector magnitude)
```

The contrast-corrected `X` is the horizontal coordinate; peak *breadth* —
side-averaged FWHM or the closed-form pseudo-Voigt integral breadth,
selectable in the original (`getFW_IB.m`), with the instrumental breadth
subtracted — is the dependent quantity fit against it.

fit to one of three selectable forms — linear (`size + strain·X`),
root-sum-square (`sqrt(size² + strain²·X²)`), or quadratic-in-X
(`size + strain·X²`). This is a known, citable method, not an
idiosyncratic one — useful confirmation for anyone deciding whether a
Python port is worth trusting.

## Two independent duplication axes

Beyond the sample/instrument duplication above, six files are `_HCP`-suffixed
near-copies of their cubic-symmetry counterparts (`getWH_HCP.m`,
`contrastHCP.m`, and four others). Symmetry handling and sample/instrument
handling are each implemented as separate copy-pasted branches. Representing
both as data rather than as which function you call removes both axes in
one design decision — likely the single highest-value structural change a
port makes over the original.

## Reproducibility of this audit

Anyone can repeat this: `dippa_v3.zip` is public and MIT-licensed at
[ThomasHSimm/DPPA](https://github.com/ThomasHSimm/DPPA). The `.mat`
inspection used `scipy.io.loadmat` on the bundled `0. variables/fit.mat`
and `genset.mat`. No proprietary or unpublished data was used.

The full write-up, including MATLAB `global`-vs-preference-store
inconsistencies and a short landscape check, is in
[`AUDIT.md`](https://github.com/ThomasHSimm/dippa/blob/main/AUDIT.md) in
the repository root.
